judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports correctly identify the config field mismatch, the SCORE-77 commit, the reproduction flaw, and the model-swap critique; they are comparable in substance and quality.

report_1.evidence: a stale key in `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` — that is the file to change. It still says `expected_label_field: label`, but commit `53551e8` renamed the gold-label field in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` from `label` to `gold_label`. ... since no record has a `label` key anymore, `.get()` silently returns `None` for every record

report_2.evidence: The file to change is `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`. It still says `expected_label_field: label`, but the eval set `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` was migrated so every record's gold-label key is now `gold_label`. ... `record.get("label")`, which returns `None` for every record

report_1.evidence: introduced by commit **`53551e8` "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"**, authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC. Evidence: `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and 0.00 on 2026-06-17

report_2.evidence: Commit `53551e8` — "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC. ... `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows 0.91/0.90/0.92 through 2026-06-16 and 0.00 from 2026-06-17 onward, the same day as the commit

report_1.evidence: it re-ran tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`, which only calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` — it never loads the config, never reads the gold label, and never computes accuracy. ... only the batch path performs that comparison. Correct reproduction: run the actual batch scorer (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`) and observe 0.00

report_2.evidence: `pipeline/score_one.py` calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` directly and never loads the config or compares against a gold label, so it exercises only the prediction half of the pipeline, which was never broken. ... Correct reproduction: run the batch scorer itself (accuracy=0.00)

report_1.evidence: The predictions were never wrong, so changing what produces them cannot fix a scoring-side field mismatch; accuracy would remain 0.0 after a rollback, wasting time and possibly casting false suspicion on the "new model." The correct action is the one-line config fix in answer 1.

report_2.evidence: flipping a model/config on the shared nightly job just to observe the effect is exactly the "flip it to see what happens in a shared environment" anti-pattern; the correct experiment is the one-line in-memory check above ... the defect is in the gold-label lookup, so accuracy would remain 0.00 under any model
